question: Which of the following best describes the topology/structure of the World Wide Web?
options: It is a complex structure containing all topology types
explanation: The WWW has a complex topology containing all types: star-shaped, grid-shaped, tree-shaped, ring-shaped, and fully-connected.
simple_explanation: Think of the web like a city road network — not a neat grid or a simple ring. It's a messy combination of all those shapes mixed together.

question: In a traditional 3-tier web architecture, which technologies run on the CLIENT tier?
explanation: The client tier (browser) runs HTML + CSS + JavaScript. The server tier runs PHP/Java/etc. and the database tier runs MySQL/Oracle/etc.
simple_explanation: A web app has 3 layers: your browser (HTML/CSS/JS), a server (PHP/Java), and a database (MySQL). The client tier is just what runs inside your browser.

question: In the separation of concerns principle applied to client-side technologies, what is the role of CSS?
explanation: In client-side technologies: HTML = content, CSS = style and appearance, JavaScript = action. This principle was introduced by Edsger Dijkstra.
simple_explanation: HTML is the words on a page, CSS makes it look nice (colours, fonts, layout), and JavaScript makes things happen when you click. CSS = looks.

question: True or False: JavaScript runs on the web server, not in the browser.
explanation: False. JavaScript is a client-side language that runs in the browser. It is part of the client tier alongside HTML and CSS.
simple_explanation: JavaScript runs in YOUR browser, not on the server. That's what makes it different from PHP, which runs on the server before sending anything to you.

question: True or False: HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol.
explanation: True. HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the protocol used for communication between web browsers and web servers.
simple_explanation: HTTP is the rulebook for how browsers and servers talk to each other. It's the reason URLs start with http:// or https://.

question:
Complete the sentence:

In the 3-tier web architecture, the tier responsible for storing data (e.g. MySQL) is called the ___ tier.
explanation: The three tiers are: Client (browser), Server (PHP/Java), and Database (MySQL/Oracle). The database tier stores and manages all persistent data.
simple_explanation: The three layers are: what you see in the browser, the server that processes things, and the database that stores everything. MySQL lives in that third database tier.

question: Which HTML attribute is used to give an element a unique identifier?
explanation: All HTML tags share these global attributes: id (unique identifier), title (tooltip text), style (inline CSS), and class (one or more CSS class names).
simple_explanation: The 'id' attribute is like a name tag — it's unique to one specific element on the page. Only one element can have a given id.

question: Which HTML tag is used to create a hyperlink?
explanation: The <a> tag creates a hyperlink. Its main attribute is href, which specifies the URL — either absolute (http://www.google.com) or relative (1/test.html).
simple_explanation: The tag stands for 'anchor'. You put the URL in its href attribute and clickable text between the tags: Click here.

question: In an HTML table, which tag is used to create a HEADER cell?
explanation: In an HTML table: <table> defines the table, <tr> defines a row, <th> defines a header cell (bold by default), and <td> defines a data cell.
simple_explanation: Think of a spreadsheet: <th> is for the column headers (bold text), <td> is for the regular data cells. <tr> wraps each row.

question: An HTML Element is defined as:
explanation:
An Element = Tag + Content. For example:

Hello

— the opening tag, the text content, and the closing tag together form the element.
simple_explanation:
A tag is the label (

), content is what's inside (Hello), and the whole thing together —

Hello

— is the element.

question:
True or False: In HTML, the
tag requires a closing tag such as
.
explanation:
False.
is a self-closing (void) tag and does not require a closing tag. It can be written as
or
in XHTML.
simple_explanation:
Some HTML tags are self-contained and don't wrap around any content — like
(line break) and . They don't need a tag.

question:
True or False: HTML tag names are case sensitive —

and

are treated as different tags.
explanation:
False. HTML tag names are case INsensitive —

and

produce the same result in browsers. However, best practice (and XHTML requirement) is always lowercase.
simple_explanation:
HTML tag names don't care about case —

and

do the same thing. However, always write tags in lowercase as good practice, and be aware XML IS case sensitive.

question:
Complete the HTML tag used to insert an image:

<___ src="photo.jpg" alt="A photo" />
explanation: The tag is used to embed images in HTML. The src attribute specifies the image path and alt provides alternative text for accessibility.
simple_explanation: is the image tag. src tells it where the image file is, and alt is the text description shown if the image fails to load.

question: An HTML hyperlink is created using the tag. What attribute specifies the destination URL? Visit AUT
explanation: The href attribute (short for Hypertext Reference) specifies the URL that the hyperlink points to. Without it, the <a> tag is just text.
simple_explanation: href stands for 'hypertext reference' — it's the attribute that tells the link WHERE to go. Without href, an <a> tag doesn't actually go anywhere.

question: What is the correct way to start a PHP code block?
explanation: PHP code blocks begin with <?php and end with ?>. These are called PHP declaration blocks.
simple_explanation: PHP code is wrapped in <?php ... ?> — like putting your code inside a special container so the server knows to run it as PHP.

question: When does PHP code execute?
options: In the browser, after the page loads
On the web server, before the page is sent to the client
On the client, as the user interacts with the page
Only when the user submits a form
explanation: PHP executes on a web server BEFORE the web page is sent to a client. The generated HTML is what gets returned to the browser.
simple_explanation: PHP runs on the server before you see anything in your browser. The server processes all the PHP first, then sends plain HTML. Your browser never sees the PHP code itself.

question: What character do PHP variable names start with?
explanation: All PHP variables start with a $ sign, for example: $votingAge, $name, $count.
simple_explanation: Every PHP variable starts with a $ sign. It's how PHP knows it's a variable and not just a word. So $name is a variable, but name is not.

question: True or False: A PHP file that contains NO PHP code should use the .php file extension.
explanation: False. A PHP file that contains no PHP code should use the .html extension. The .php extension tells the server to process the file through the PHP engine, which is unnecessary overhead if there's no PHP in it.
simple_explanation: If a file has no PHP code in it, call it .html. Only use .php when the file actually contains <?php code ?>. Using .php on a plain HTML file wastes server resources.

question: True or False: PHP variables are case-sensitive — $Name and $name are treated as different variables.
explanation: True. PHP variable names are case-sensitive. $Name, $name, and $NAME are three completely different variables.
simple_explanation: In PHP, $Name and $name are two completely different variables. A very common source of bugs for beginners — always use consistent capitalisation.

question:
What is the opening tag used to start a PHP code block?

___
  echo "Hello World";
?>
explanation: PHP code blocks are opened with <?php and closed with ?>. Everything between these tags is processed as PHP by the server.
simple_explanation: PHP starts with <?php and ends with ?>. Think of them like brackets — everything inside gets run as PHP code on the server.

question:
What will the following PHP code output? echo '

The legal voting age is $votingAge.

';
explanation: Single quotation marks in PHP print everything literally — the variable name '$votingAge' is printed as text, not its value.
simple_explanation: Single quotes in PHP are 'what you see is what you get'. They print everything exactly as typed, including the dollar sign. No magic happens.

question:
What will the following PHP code output if $votingAge = 18? echo "

The legal voting age is $votingAge.

";
explanation: Double quotation marks in PHP interpolate variables — $votingAge is replaced with its value (18) in the output.
simple_explanation: Double quotes are 'smart' — PHP looks inside them for $ variables and swaps them for their actual values. Single quotes don't do this.

question: True or False: In PHP, variables inside single-quoted strings have their values substituted automatically.
explanation: False. Variable substitution (interpolation) only happens inside double-quoted strings. Inside single quotes, the variable name is printed literally.
simple_explanation: Only double quotes do the variable swap trick. Single quotes are dumb — they print exactly what you type, dollar sign and all.

question:
In PHP, the string concatenation operator is a single character. What is it?

$fullName = $firstName ___ $lastName;
explanation: PHP uses the dot (.) operator to concatenate (join) strings. For example: $full = $first . " " . $last;
simple_explanation: PHP joins strings with a dot. So 'Hello' . ' ' . 'World' gives you 'Hello World'. It's different from JavaScript which uses + for this.

question: What are the two types of PHP arrays?
explanation: PHP has indexed arrays (accessed by a numeric index, starting at 0) and associative arrays (accessed by a named key).
simple_explanation: Indexed arrays are like a numbered list (item 0, 1, 2...). Associative arrays are like a dictionary — each item has a name (key) instead of a number.

question: True or False: In PHP, array indexes start at 1 by default.
explanation: False. PHP array indexes start at 0 by default, just like most programming languages. The first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on.
simple_explanation: Arrays in PHP (and most languages) start counting from 0, not 1. So the first item is [0], the second is [1], and so on. This trips up a lot of beginners.

question:
Complete the PHP code to create an indexed array:

$colours = ___("red", "green", "blue");
explanation: The array() constructor creates an array in PHP. This is the syntax taught in this course. Modern PHP also allows $colours = ["red", "green", "blue"]; as a shorthand.
simple_explanation: To create an array in PHP, you use array() and list your items inside separated by commas. Think of it like filling a box with labelled slots.

question: Which PHP operator type compares operands and returns a Boolean value?
explanation: Comparison operators compare operands and return a Boolean value (true/false). Examples: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
simple_explanation: Comparison operators ask a yes/no question — like 'is 5 greater than 3?' and return true or false.

question: What does the += operator do in PHP?
options: Checks if two values are equal
Adds the right operand to the left operand and assigns the result back
Divides the left operand by the right operand
Appends a string to another string
explanation: += is the compound addition assignment operator. It adds the right operand to the left operand and assigns the new value back to the left operand.
simple_explanation: $x += 5 is shorthand for $x = $x + 5. It takes whatever $x currently is, adds 5 to it, and saves the result back into $x.

question: True or False: In PHP, == and === are exactly the same operator.
explanation: False. == checks value equality only (1 == '1' is true). === checks both value AND type (1 === '1' is false because one is an integer and the other is a string).
simple_explanation: == just checks if the values match. === is stricter — it also checks that they're the same TYPE. So 1 == '1' is true, but 1 === '1' is false.

explanation: %= is the compound modulus assignment operator. 10 % 3 = 1 (the remainder when 10 is divided by 3). So $x becomes 1.
simple_explanation: The % operator finds the remainder after division. 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1. So 10 % 3 = 1. The %= just saves that result back into $x.

question: What does the $_GET superglobal contain?
options: An array of values passed via HTTP cookies
An array of values from a form submitted with the GET method
explanation: $_GET contains an array of values from a form submitted with the GET method. $_POST does the same for POST.
simple_explanation: $_GET grabs data sent via the URL. When you search Google and see ?q=cats in the address bar, PHP would read that using $_GET['q'].

question: Which superglobal array contains values from BOTH $_GET and $_POST (and cookies)?
explanation: $_REQUEST is an array of all elements found in $_COOKIE, $_GET, and $_POST arrays combined.
simple_explanation: $_REQUEST is the lazy option — it combines $_GET, $_POST, and $_COOKIE into one array so you don't have to care how the data was sent.

question: True or False: $_SESSION data is automatically available across all pages without any setup code.
explanation: False. You must call session_start() at the top of every page that needs to use session data. Without it, $_SESSION will not be accessible.
simple_explanation: Sessions don't just work magically. You have to write session_start(); at the top of each PHP page before you can read or write any $_SESSION variables.

question:
Complete the PHP code to start a session:

___()
explanation: session_start() must be called before any output is sent to the browser. It initialises the session and makes $_SESSION available.
simple_explanation: You call session_start() at the very top of your PHP file to 'open' the session. After that you can read and write to $_SESSION like any other array.

question: Which PHP function is used to test whether a variable contains a numeric string?
explanation: is_numeric() tests whether a variable contains a numeric string or value. Commonly used to validate form input before calculations.
simple_explanation: is_numeric() asks 'is this a number?' — useful when someone fills in a form and you want to make sure they typed a number before doing maths with it.

question: True or False: empty() returns true when a variable contains the string "0".
explanation: True. PHP's empty() considers these values as empty: "", 0, 0.0, "0", null, false, and empty arrays. This is a common gotcha when validating form input.
simple_explanation: empty() is a bit aggressive — it considers "0" (the string zero) as empty, which can cause bugs if someone legitimately enters 0 in a form field. Watch out for this.

question:
To retrieve the value of a form field named 'email' submitted via the POST method, you would write:

$email = ___['email'];
explanation: $_POST is the superglobal array that holds all form values submitted using the POST method. Access individual fields using their name attribute as the key.
simple_explanation: When an HTML form uses method="post", PHP receives the data in $_POST. The key is whatever you named the input field in HTML.

question: How many parameters does the substr() function take?
explanation: substr() takes three parameters: (1) the string, (2) the start position, (3) the length to select. The third parameter is optional — omitting it selects to the end of the string.
simple_explanation: substr() cuts a piece out of a string: which string, where to start cutting, and how many characters to take.

question: True or False: In PHP, string indexes start at 0.
explanation: True. Just like arrays, string character positions in PHP start at index 0. So in 'Hello', 'H' is at position 0 and 'e' is at position 1.
simple_explanation: The first character in a string is always at position 0. So substr('Hello', 0, 1) gives you 'H', and substr('Hello', 1, 1) gives you 'e'.

question:
What function returns the number of characters in a PHP string?

$len = ___("Hello World"); // returns 11
explanation: strlen() returns the length of a string (number of characters, including spaces). strlen("Hello World") returns 11.
simple_explanation: strlen() counts the characters in a string — including spaces. It's like asking 'how long is this text?' strlen stands for 'string length'.

question: What does ASCII stand for?
options: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
Advanced System Code for Internet Interchange
Automated Standard Code for Information Input
explanation: ASCII stands for American Standard Code for Information Interchange. It provides numeric representations of English characters, with values ranging from 0 to 255.
simple_explanation: ASCII is a system that gives every letter, number, and symbol a unique number so computers can store and compare them. 'A' is 65, 'a' is 97.

question: True or False: In ASCII, uppercase letters have higher numeric values than lowercase letters.
explanation: False. Uppercase letters (65–90) have LOWER numeric values than lowercase letters (97–122). This means 'a' > 'A' when comparing strings.
simple_explanation: It's the opposite — lowercase letters have the higher numbers in ASCII. 'a' is 97 and 'A' is only 65. So lowercase is 'greater than' uppercase.

question: What is the ASCII numeric value of the uppercase letter 'A'?
explanation: The uppercase letter 'A' has ASCII value 65. Uppercase letters run from 65 ('A') to 90 ('Z'), and lowercase letters run from 97 ('a') to 122 ('z').
simple_explanation: 'A' = 65, 'B' = 66, all the way to 'Z' = 90. Then lowercase starts further up: 'a' = 97. Knowing this helps you understand how string comparisons work.

question: When does the else clause execute in an if...else statement?
options: When the if condition is true
When the if condition is false
Only when the if block has an error
explanation: The else clause executes when the condition in the if...else statement evaluates to FALSE.
simple_explanation: Think of it like: 'if it's raining, take an umbrella — else, leave it at home.' The else only happens when the if condition wasn't true.

question: What is the key difference between a while loop and a do...while loop?
options: while loops run faster than do...while loops
do...while loops cannot use a counter variable
A do...while loop always executes at least once before checking the condition
A while loop can only count up, not down
explanation: do...while statements always execute once before the conditional expression is evaluated. A while loop checks the condition BEFORE each iteration.
simple_explanation: A while loop checks first, then runs. A do...while runs first, then checks. The do...while is GUARANTEED to execute at least once.

explanation: The loop runs while $count <= 5, starting at 1 and incrementing with $count++. It executes for values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — a total of 5 times.
simple_explanation: Start at 1, keep going while ≤ 5 — that's 5 rounds. When $count becomes 6, the condition (6 ≤ 5) is false and the loop stops.

question: True or False: A for loop in PHP can only count upwards (increment), not downwards (decrement).
explanation: False. A for loop can count in any direction. You can decrement with $i-- just as easily as you increment with $i++.
simple_explanation: You can make a for loop count down just as easily as up. for ($i = 10; $i >= 0; $i--) counts from 10 down to 0. The direction depends on what you write.

question: True or False: The foreach loop in PHP is used specifically for iterating over arrays.
explanation: True. foreach is designed specifically for iterating over arrays (and objects). It automatically steps through each element without needing a counter variable.
simple_explanation: foreach is the easy way to loop through an array — it goes through each item one by one automatically. You don't need to manage a counter like in a for loop.

explanation: The foreach loop syntax is: foreach ($array as $value) { ... }. It iterates over every element in the array, assigning each element to $value in turn.
simple_explanation: foreach is like saying 'for each item in this list, do something with it'. PHP handles all the counting — you just say what to do with each item.

explanation: continue skips the remaining code in the current loop iteration and jumps to the next iteration. This code would print 0,1,2,3,4,6,7,8,9 — skipping 5.
simple_explanation: continue is like saying 'skip this one and move on'. When the loop hits 5, it skips the echo and goes straight to 6. The loop doesn't stop — it just skips that round.

question: Which SQL command is used to retrieve data from a database table?
explanation: SELECT is the SQL command used to query and retrieve data from a database. Basic syntax: SELECT column FROM table WHERE condition;
simple_explanation: SELECT is how you ask the database 'give me this data'. Like SELECT * FROM students; means 'give me everything from the students table'.

question: Which SQL command is used to add a new row of data to a table?
explanation: INSERT INTO is used to add new rows to a table. Syntax: INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2);
simple_explanation: INSERT INTO puts new data into a table. Think of it like filling in a new row in a spreadsheet — you say which table, which columns, and what values to put in.

question: Which SQL command is used to modify existing records in a table?
explanation: UPDATE is used to change existing data in a table. Syntax: UPDATE table SET column = value WHERE condition; Always use WHERE or you'll update every row.
simple_explanation: UPDATE edits existing rows. Like crossing out old info and writing new info. Always use WHERE to specify which rows — otherwise you change EVERYTHING in the table.

question: In MySQL, what does AUTO_INCREMENT do when applied to a column?
options: Adds 10 to the value each time
Automatically assigns the next available integer value when a new row is inserted
explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT automatically assigns an incrementing integer to a column each time a new row is inserted. It's commonly used for primary key columns (id fields).
simple_explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT is like an automatic row numbering system. Every time you add a new record, it gets the next number (1, 2, 3...) without you having to specify it.

question: Which MySQL data type would you use to store a short piece of text up to 255 characters?
explanation: VARCHAR(n) stores variable-length strings up to n characters. It's efficient because it only uses as much space as the data requires, up to the maximum.
simple_explanation: VARCHAR is for text like names, emails, or titles. The number in brackets is the maximum length. VARCHAR(255) can hold up to 255 characters but only uses what it needs.

question: True or False: If you run UPDATE without a WHERE clause, only the first row in the table is updated.
explanation: False. Without a WHERE clause, UPDATE modifies EVERY row in the table. This is one of the most dangerous mistakes in SQL — always double-check your WHERE clause.
simple_explanation: Without WHERE, UPDATE changes absolutely everything. If you forget WHERE on UPDATE students SET grade = 'F', every single student gets an F. Always use WHERE!

question: True or False: A PRIMARY KEY column in MySQL must contain unique values and cannot be NULL.
explanation: True. A PRIMARY KEY uniquely identifies each row. It must be unique (no two rows have the same value) and cannot be NULL. It's typically an auto-incrementing integer.
simple_explanation: The primary key is the unique ID for each row — like a student ID number. No two rows can have the same value, and it can never be left blank (NULL).

question: True or False: In PHP, you use mysqli_fetch_assoc() to retrieve a result row as an associative array.
explanation: True. mysqli_fetch_assoc() returns the next row from a result set as an associative array, where the keys are the column names. This is the standard pattern used in this course.
simple_explanation: mysqli_fetch_assoc() grabs one row at a time from your query results and gives it to you as an array where the keys match your column names — so $row['name'] gives you the name column.

question:
Complete the SQL query to select ALL columns from a table called 'bookings':

SELECT ___ FROM bookings;
explanation: The asterisk (*) is a wildcard that means 'all columns'. SELECT * FROM bookings; retrieves every column for every row in the bookings table.
simple_explanation: The * means 'everything'. SELECT * is like saying 'give me all the columns'. It's the easiest way to see all data in a table, though in practice you'd name specific columns.

question:
Complete the SQL statement to delete all rows where status = 'cancelled':

___ FROM bookings WHERE status = 'cancelled';
explanation: DELETE FROM table WHERE condition; removes rows matching the condition. Without WHERE, all rows would be deleted. Always verify your WHERE clause before running DELETE.
simple_explanation: DELETE removes rows from a table. You need WHERE to say which rows — otherwise you delete everything. Think before you run a DELETE statement!

question:
Complete the PHP code to connect to a MySQL database using mysqli:

$conn = ___("localhost", "username", "password", "dbname");
explanation: mysqli_connect() establishes a connection to a MySQL database. It takes four parameters: host, username, password, and database name. The result is a connection object used in all subsequent queries.
simple_explanation: mysqli_connect() is like dialling a phone call to your database. You give it the server address, your login details, and which database to use. Store the result in $conn and use it for all your queries.

question:
In PHP with mysqli, what function do you call to run a SQL query?

$result = ___(  $conn, "SELECT * FROM users" );
explanation: mysqli_query() executes a SQL statement against the database. It takes the connection object and the SQL string as arguments, and returns a result object for SELECT queries.
simple_explanation: mysqli_query() sends your SQL to the database and gets the answer back. You give it the connection ($conn) and the SQL statement, and it gives you the results.

question: In the Ajax Technology Family, which role does 'fetch' play?
explanation: In the Ajax Technology Family: HTML+CSS = presentation, DOM = dynamic display, JSON = data interchange, fetch = asynchronous data retrieval, JavaScript = binding everything together.
simple_explanation: fetch is the modern JavaScript tool for requesting data from a server in the background — without reloading the page.

question: What does DOM stand for?
explanation: DOM stands for Document Object Model. It is used for dynamic display and interaction on the client, and for managing JSON/XML on both client and server.
simple_explanation: The DOM is like a live map of your webpage that JavaScript can read and change. When you click a button and text appears without reloading — that's JavaScript editing the DOM.

explanation: In JavaScript, object properties are accessed with dot notation: objectName.propertyName. So who.name accesses the 'name' property, which holds 'Chuck'.
simple_explanation: In JavaScript, to get a property from an object you write: objectName.propertyName. So who.name gives you whatever is in the 'name' field — which is 'Chuck'.

question: True or False: JSON keys must always be enclosed in double quotes.
explanation: True. Unlike JavaScript objects (which allow unquoted keys), valid JSON requires all keys to be enclosed in double quotes. This is a strict requirement of the JSON specification.
simple_explanation: In JSON, the keys MUST have double quotes around them — like {"name": "Chuck"}. This makes JSON stricter than regular JavaScript objects, but it's also why it's predictable.

question: True or False: XML tags are case-sensitive — <Name> and <name> would be treated as different tags.
explanation: True. XML is strictly case-sensitive. <Name>, <name>, and <NAME> are all completely different tags. Unlike HTML, XML has no tolerance for case mismatches.
simple_explanation: XML is strict about case — <Name> and <name> are completely different tags to XML. HTML is more forgiving, but XML is not. This is one reason JSON became more popular — fewer strict rules.

question:
In JavaScript, what method converts a JSON string into a JavaScript object?

const obj = JSON.___(jsonString);
explanation: JSON.parse() converts a JSON-formatted string into a JavaScript object. The reverse operation (object to string) is JSON.stringify().
simple_explanation: JSON.parse() unwraps a JSON string and turns it into a real JavaScript object you can work with. Think of it as 'opening the package'. JSON.stringify() is the reverse — it wraps the object back up.

question:
What does WSDL stand for?

W___ S___ D___ L___
explanation: WSDL stands for Web Service Description Language. It describes what operations a web service provides and what data it expects — essentially a contract for the service.
simple_explanation: WSDL is like a menu for a web service — it describes what the service can do, what info you send it, and what you get back.

question: Which term best describes how AJAX handles user interactions on a web page?
explanation: The Ajax application model is a 'partial screen update' model — only the interface elements with new information are updated; the rest stays as-is.
simple_explanation: AJAX only updates the part of the page that changed. Like on Facebook — when a new comment appears, the whole page doesn't reload. Just that section updates quietly.

question: True or False: AJAX requires the entire page to reload every time new data is fetched from the server.
explanation: False. AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML) specifically exists to avoid full page reloads. It fetches data in the background and updates only the relevant part of the DOM.
simple_explanation: AJAX's whole purpose is to NOT reload the page. It sends a request quietly in the background and updates just the part of the page that needs new info.

question: True or False: fetch() in JavaScript returns a Promise.
explanation: True. fetch() is asynchronous and returns a Promise. You handle the result using .then() or async/await syntax.
simple_explanation: fetch() doesn't give you the data instantly — it gives you a Promise, which is like a 'IOU' that will be filled in when the data arrives. You use .then() to say what to do with it.

question:
Complete the fetch call to request data from a PHP file:

___("data.php")
  .then(response => response.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data));
explanation: fetch() is the modern JavaScript API for making HTTP requests. It replaces the older XMLHttpRequest (XHR) approach and returns a Promise.
simple_explanation: fetch() is how JavaScript asks the server for data without reloading the page. You give it a URL, it goes and gets the data, and .then() tells it what to do with the response.

question: What does CSS stand for?
explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets. It is the style language of the Web, used to present the content of web pages.
simple_explanation: CSS = Cascading Style Sheets. It's what makes websites look nice — colours, fonts, layout. Without it, every page would just be plain black text.

question: What is the correct syntax for a CSS rule?
explanation: A CSS rule consists of a selector followed by curly braces containing property-value pairs separated by colons and terminated with semicolons: selector { property: value; }
simple_explanation: CSS rules look like: h1 { color: red; } — the selector picks which HTML elements to style, and inside the curly braces you list what to change and what to change it to.

question: Which of the following is the BEST practice for applying CSS to a website?
options: External stylesheet linked with a <link> element
Using the style attribute on the <body> tag only
explanation: External stylesheets are best practice. The same .css file can be linked and reused across many pages. Inline styles and internal stylesheets should be avoided as they are harder to maintain.
simple_explanation: External CSS files are best because one file styles your whole website. Change it once and every page updates. Inline styles are messy and hard to manage.

question: Which CSS selector targets an element with a specific id attribute?
explanation: In CSS, the # symbol targets an element by its id attribute. For example, #danger { color: red; } applies to the element with id="danger".
simple_explanation: # = id selector. .danger targets class="danger", but #danger targets id="danger". IDs are unique — only one element per page should have a given id.

question: Which CSS property is used to make a container use Flexbox layout?
explanation: Setting display: flex on a container element activates Flexbox layout. Items inside the container then flexibly flow and can be aligned using justify-content and align-items.
simple_explanation: To turn on Flexbox, add display: flex to the container div. All the elements inside it become 'flex items' and you can easily align them in rows or columns.

question: True or False: An inline style will override styles defined in an external stylesheet.
explanation: True. Based on the cascading order, inline styles are the most specific and will override CSS from external or internal stylesheets.
simple_explanation: Inline styles win the priority battle. CSS has a specificity system — the more targeted (specific) a style is, the higher priority it gets. Inline beats everything.

question:
True or False: In CSS, the selector 'div p' selects all

elements that are direct children of a

only.
explanation:
False. The selector 'div p' selects ALL

elements that are descendants of a

(not just direct children). The direct child selector would be 'div > p'.
simple_explanation:
'div p' means any

that's inside a

at any depth — grandchildren, great-grandchildren, etc. If you only want direct children, use 'div > p' instead.

question:
Complete the CSS rule to change the colour of all h1 elements to blue:

h1 { ___: blue; }
explanation: The CSS 'color' property sets the text colour of an element. CSS uses American spelling (color, not colour).
simple_explanation: The CSS property for text colour is 'color' (American spelling). So h1 { color: blue; } makes all h1 headings blue.

question:
Complete the HTML tag to link an external CSS file named 'styles.css':

<link rel="stylesheet" type="text/css" ___="styles.css" />
explanation: The href attribute in the <link> tag specifies the path to the external CSS file, just like in the <a> anchor tag.
simple_explanation: href tells the browser where to find the CSS file. It's the same attribute used in hyperlinks — it means 'here is the reference/path'.

question: Which PHP function is used to test whether a string matches a regular expression pattern?
explanation: preg_match() tests whether a string matches a pattern. preg_replace() replaces matches, and preg_split() splits a string by a pattern.
simple_explanation: preg_match() is your 'does this match?' function. Give it a pattern and a string — it returns true if the pattern is found in the string, false if not.

question: In a PHP regular expression, what does the \d character class match?
explanation: \d is a shortcut for [0-9] and matches any single digit. \w matches word characters [A-Za-z0-9_], and \s matches whitespace (space or tab).
simple_explanation: \d = digit (0–9). Think 'd for digit'. \w = word character (letters, digits, underscore). \s = space. These shortcuts save you writing out [0-9] every time.

question: What does the regular expression pattern /^WebDev$/ match?
options: The exact string 'WebDev' and nothing else
explanation: ^ anchors to the start of the string and $ anchors to the end. Together they mean the entire string must exactly equal 'WebDev' — nothing before or after.
simple_explanation: ^ means 'start here', $ means 'end here'. So ^WebDev$ means the string must START with WebDev AND END with WebDev — it can't have anything else around it.

question: What does the quantifier + mean in a regular expression?
options: Zero or one of the preceding element
Zero or more of the preceding element
One or more of the preceding element
Exactly two of the preceding element
explanation: In regex: ? = zero or one, * = zero or more, + = one or more. So /bana+na/ matches 'banana' and 'banaana' but NOT 'banna' because + requires at least one 'a'.
simple_explanation: + means 'at least one'. So a+ needs at least one 'a' — it matches 'a', 'aa', 'aaa' etc. Compare to * which allows zero or more (so it would also match nothing).

question: True or False: The /i modifier in a PHP regular expression makes the pattern case-insensitive.
explanation: True. The /i modifier makes the pattern case-insensitive, so /webdev/i would match 'WebDev', 'WEBDEV', 'webdev', etc.
simple_explanation: Adding /i after your pattern makes it ignore upper/lowercase. So /hello/i matches 'Hello', 'HELLO', 'hElLo' — any capitalisation.

question: True or False: The \W character class (uppercase W) matches the same characters as \w.
explanation: False. Uppercase forms are the complement (opposite). \w matches word characters [A-Za-z0-9_], while \W matches any character that is NOT in that set (symbols, punctuation, spaces etc.).
simple_explanation: Uppercase = opposite. \w matches letters/digits/underscore. \W (uppercase) matches everything ELSE — like punctuation and symbols. Same rule applies for \D (not a digit) and \S (not whitespace).

question:
In PHP regular expressions, what function would you use to replace all matches of a pattern in a string?

$result = ___(pattern, replacement, string);
explanation: preg_replace() replaces all occurrences of the pattern in the string with the replacement string. It's the regex equivalent of str_replace() but with pattern matching.
simple_explanation: preg_replace() is like Find & Replace but with regex patterns. You give it what to find (pattern), what to replace it with, and which string to search in.

question:
What regex special character matches the END of a string?

/WebDev___/  — matches only strings ending with 'WebDev'
explanation: $ anchors the pattern to the end of the string. So /WebDev$/ matches 'I love WebDev' but not 'WebDev is great!' (because there's more text after WebDev).
simple_explanation: $ means 'this must be at the end'. So /bye$/ matches 'goodbye' but not 'bye for now' — because 'bye' isn't the last thing in that second string.

question: Which PHP function returns the number of rows returned by a SELECT query?
explanation: mysqli_num_rows() returns the number of rows in a SELECT query result. For INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE queries that don't return results, use mysqli_affected_rows() instead.
simple_explanation: mysqli_num_rows() tells you how many rows came back from a SELECT. For INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (which don't return rows), you'd use mysqli_affected_rows() instead.

question: What does the @ operator do when placed before a PHP function call like @mysqli_connect(...)?
options: Suppresses any error messages the function would generate
Forces the function to return true
Logs errors to a file instead of displaying them
explanation: The @ error control operator suppresses error messages from a function. This is used so database error details (like passwords) are not shown to users.
simple_explanation: @ before a function silences any error messages it would normally print. Useful for database connections — you don't want users seeing 'wrong password' error messages with your DB credentials.

question: What is the correct order of steps when accessing a MySQL database from PHP?
explanation: The three-step pattern is: (1) Open the connection with mysqli_connect(), (2) Execute SQL with mysqli_query(), (3) Close with mysqli_free_result() and mysqli_close().
simple_explanation: Think of it like a phone call: dial (connect), talk (query), hang up (close). You always connect first, do your database work, then clean up and close the connection.

question: True or False: mysqli_fetch_assoc() and mysqli_fetch_row() both return one row at a time, but mysqli_fetch_assoc() uses column names as keys while mysqli_fetch_row() uses numeric indexes.
explanation: True. Both move through the result set one row at a time. mysqli_fetch_row() gives you $row[0], $row[1] etc., while mysqli_fetch_assoc() gives you $row['name'], $row['email'] etc.
simple_explanation: Both grab one row at a time. The difference is how you access the data: fetch_row uses numbers ($row[0]), fetch_assoc uses column names ($row['name']). fetch_assoc is usually easier to read.

question: True or False: Running DELETE FROM users; without a WHERE clause will delete only the first row.
explanation: False. DELETE without WHERE deletes ALL records in the table. This is one of the most dangerous mistakes in SQL — always double-check your WHERE clause before running DELETE.
simple_explanation: No WHERE = delete everything. DELETE FROM users; wipes out every single user in your database instantly. Always write the WHERE clause first before typing DELETE.

question:
Complete the PHP code to close a mysqli database connection:

___(  $conn  );
explanation: mysqli_close() closes the connection to the MySQL database server. It's good practice to call this when finished, though PHP automatically closes open connections at the end of a script.
simple_explanation: mysqli_close() hangs up the phone call to the database. PHP will close it automatically when the script ends anyway, but calling it explicitly is good clean coding practice.

explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT tells MySQL to automatically assign the next available integer when a new row is inserted. Combined with PRIMARY KEY, this creates a unique auto-numbered ID for each record.
simple_explanation: AUTO_INCREMENT means MySQL handles the numbering for you. Insert a row and it gets ID 1, next row gets ID 2, and so on — you never have to specify the ID yourself.

question:
Complete the SQL query to retrieve only students with a grade above 80, sorted by name:

SELECT * FROM students WHERE grade > 80 ___ name;
explanation: ORDER BY sorts the results. You can sort by one or more columns, ascending (default) or descending (add DESC). Without ORDER BY, the order of results is not guaranteed.
simple_explanation: ORDER BY is how you sort your results. ORDER BY name sorts alphabetically A-Z. Add DESC after for Z-A. You can sort by numbers too — ORDER BY grade DESC gives highest grades first.

question: What is a PRIMARY KEY in a relational database?
options: A field that stores the most important data in each row
A field that contains a unique identifier for each record in a table
A field that links two tables together
The first column in any database table
explanation: A primary key is a field (or combination of fields) that contains a unique identifier for each record. No two rows can have the same primary key value, and it cannot be NULL.
simple_explanation: A primary key is the unique ID for each row — like a student ID or passport number. No two rows can share the same value, and it can never be blank.

question: What is a FOREIGN KEY in a relational database?
options: A key imported from another database system
A primary key that is automatically generated
A field in a related table that refers to the primary key of another table
A field that stores encrypted data
explanation: A foreign key is a field in a related (child) table that references the primary key in a primary (parent) table. Foreign keys link records across multiple tables.
simple_explanation: A foreign key is how you link two tables. If an Orders table has a customer_id column that matches the id in a Customers table, that customer_id is a foreign key.

question: True or False: A flat-file database stores information in a single table, while a relational database stores information across multiple related tables.
explanation: True. A flat-file database is a single table (like a spreadsheet). A relational database uses multiple tables linked by primary and foreign keys to avoid data duplication.
simple_explanation: Flat-file = one big spreadsheet with everything. Relational = multiple connected tables. Relational is better for large data because you don't repeat the same info over and over.

question: True or False: SQL is case-sensitive — SELECT must be written in uppercase.
explanation: False. SQL keywords are NOT case-sensitive. 'SELECT', 'select', and 'Select' all work the same way. However, convention is to write SQL keywords in uppercase for readability.
simple_explanation: SQL keywords like SELECT, FROM, WHERE don't care about case — select, SELECT, and Select all work. It's just convention to write them in uppercase so they stand out.

question: Which statement best describes the relationship between the Internet and the World Wide Web?
options: They are exactly the same thing
The Web is a way of accessing information over the Internet
The Internet is part of the Web only
explanation: The lecture explains that the Internet is an interconnected network of computers, while the World Wide Web is a way of accessing information over the medium of the Internet using HTTP.
simple_explanation: The Internet is the network. The Web is one service that runs on top of it. You use the Web to access pages and data through the Internet.

question: Which HTTP method is typically used when sending form data in the request body?
explanation: The Week 1 lecture states that POST is used when you want to send data to the server, such as form data or file updates, and that the data is sent in the request body.
simple_explanation: POST is used when you want to send data to the server in the message body instead of putting it in the URL.

question: True or False: In an HTTP GET request, parameters are commonly placed in the URL as a query string.
explanation: The lecture shows that GET requests can send parameters through the URL, using a query string made up of key-value pairs separated by '&'.
simple_explanation: GET usually sends data in the URL itself, like ?name=Sam&age=20. That part is called the query string.

question:
What HTTP status code usually means the request was successful?

HTTP/1.0 ___ OK
explanation: The Week 1 HTTP response slide lists 'HTTP/1.0 200 OK' as the successful response example.
simple_explanation: 200 means the request worked successfully. It is the normal 'everything is fine' response code.

question: Which HTML element must appear inside the <head> element according to the lecture?
explanation: The Week 1 lecture states that the <head> element must contain a <title> element, whose content is displayed in the browser's title bar and bookmark list.
simple_explanation: The head section must include a title. That is the text shown on the browser tab.

question: All HTML documents must use the <___> element as the root element.
explanation: The lecture notes say that all HTML documents must use the <html> element as the root element, which contains all the other elements in the document.
simple_explanation: The whole HTML page sits inside the html element. It is the root of the document.

question: Besides echo, which PHP language construct can also be used to display output to the client?
explanation: The Week 2 lecture explains that the results of PHP processing can be returned to the client using either the echo statement or the print statement.
simple_explanation: Both echo and print can display output in PHP. They are built-in language constructs for sending text to the browser.

question: True or False: A PHP variable name can begin with a number as long as it includes a $ sign.
explanation: The Week 2 lecture says a variable name must begin with a dollar sign and can include letters, numbers, or underscores, but it cannot start with a number.
simple_explanation: Even though PHP variables start with $, the name after it still cannot begin with a number.

explanation: The Week 2 lecture states that constants are created with the define() function.
simple_explanation: If you want to make a constant in PHP, you use define().

question: True or False: It is good practice to call session_start() before sending any output to the browser.
explanation: The Week 3 lecture specifically notes that it is good practice to call session_start() before you send the Web browser any output.
simple_explanation: Start the session first, before printing HTML or text. That helps session handling work properly.

question: Which PHP function removes the first element from the beginning of an array?
explanation: The Week 3 lecture lists array_shift() as the function that removes the first element from the beginning of an array.
simple_explanation: array_shift() removes the first item in the array.

question: Which PHP function adds one or more elements to the end of an array?
explanation: The Week 3 lecture states that array_push() adds one or more elements to the end of an array.
simple_explanation: array_push() puts new items onto the end of an array.

question: Which PHP function returns true if a given value exists in an array?
explanation: The Week 3 lecture explains that in_array() returns a Boolean value of true if a given value exists in an array.
simple_explanation: Use in_array() when you want to check whether a value is somewhere inside an array.

question:
In an associative array, the symbol used between the key and value is ___ .

$age = array("Peter" ___ "35");
explanation: The Week 3 lecture shows associative arrays using the key => value syntax, for example: $age = array("Peter" => "35").
simple_explanation: Associative arrays match a key to a value using the => symbol.

question: Which PHP function can be used as another option to test whether a variable has been set?
explanation: The Week 3 lecture says that isset() is another option for checking whether a variable is set when validating form data or working with sessions.
simple_explanation: isset() checks whether a variable exists and has been set.

question: According to ASCII values, which character is considered greater when comparing strings?
options: Uppercase letters are greater than lowercase letters
Lowercase letters are greater than uppercase letters
explanation: The Week 3 lecture explains that lowercase letters have higher ASCII values than uppercase letters, so they are evaluated as greater in string comparisons.
simple_explanation: In ASCII, lowercase letters have bigger number values than uppercase letters, so lowercase counts as greater.

question: What does REST stand for?
explanation: The Week 5 lecture states that REST stands for Representational State Transfer.
simple_explanation: REST is short for Representational State Transfer. It is the resource-based style used for many web APIs.

question: What does SOAP stand for?
explanation: The Week 5 lecture identifies SOAP as Simple Object Access Protocol.
simple_explanation: SOAP is short for Simple Object Access Protocol.

question: True or False: REST treats remote services as resources and commonly uses HTTP methods such as GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE.
explanation: The Week 5 lecture explains that REST treats remote services as resources and uses standard CRUD-style HTTP methods like GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE.
simple_explanation: REST works by treating things as resources and using normal HTTP methods to retrieve, create, update, or delete them.

question:
What JavaScript method converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string?

const jsonText = JSON.___(obj);
explanation: The Week 5 lecture notes explain JSON.parse() for converting from JSON text to an object, and the reverse operation is JSON.stringify().
simple_explanation: JSON.stringify() wraps a JavaScript object up into JSON text.

question: Which statement about XML is correct?
options: XML uses only predefined tags like HTML
XML lets you define your own tags
explanation: The Week 5 lecture says XML is a meta-markup language that allows authors to customise their own elements and structure data using their own tags.
simple_explanation: Unlike HTML, XML lets you create your own tag names to describe data.

question: True or False: All XML documents must be well-formed.
explanation: The Week 5 lecture states that all XML documents need to be well-formed, while validation against a schema or DTD is optional.
simple_explanation: Every XML document must follow the basic XML rules. That is what 'well-formed' means.

question:
Which HTTP method is used in REST for a partial update?

The method is ___ .
explanation: The Week 5 lecture lists PATCH as the REST method used for a partial update.
simple_explanation: PATCH is used when you only want to update part of a resource instead of replacing the whole thing.

question: Which older browser technology does fetch() commonly replace for asynchronous requests?
explanation: The Week 5 lecture explains that fetch() is one option for implementing the Ajax model and contrasts it with the older XMLHttpRequest object.
simple_explanation: fetch() is the newer way to make background requests. It commonly replaces XMLHttpRequest.

question: True or False: In the Web 2.0 application model, the original web page remains displayed while asynchronous processing happens in the background.
explanation: The Week 5 lecture states that in the Web 2.0 model the original web page remains displayed, while messages are sent asynchronously and only relevant sections are updated when results come back.
simple_explanation: The page stays there while the browser quietly talks to the server in the background. Then only the needed parts update.

explanation: == checks value only (loose). "2" == 2 is TRUE — PHP converts the string. === checks value AND type (strict). "2" === 2 is FALSE — string vs integer. So only "1" prints.
simple_explanation: == is lenient (ignores type), === is strict (type must match too). "2" the string equals 2 loosely, but not strictly. Only the first if runs.

question:
What HTTP status code is used for a successful request?

Status code: ___
explanation: HTTP 200 OK means the request succeeded and the server returned the resource. Other codes: 404 = Not Found, 301 = Redirect, 500 = Server Error.
simple_explanation: 200 = everything worked. The server is saying 'yes, here is what you asked for'. You see this in DevTools on every successful page load.

question:
A table called 'people' has columns: id, firstname, lastname, age, email.

Which SQL query selects only first names and emails, sorted by highest age first?
options: SELECT * FROM people ORDER BY age DESC
SELECT firstname, email FROM people GROUP BY age
SELECT firstname, email FROM people ORDER BY age DESC
SELECT firstname, email FROM people ORDER BY age ASC
explanation: SELECT firstname, email picks only those columns. ORDER BY age DESC sorts descending (highest first). GROUP BY is for aggregation not sorting. SELECT * returns all columns.
simple_explanation: SELECT picks columns, ORDER BY sorts, DESC = highest first. GROUP BY is different — it groups rows for aggregate functions like COUNT. Don't mix them up.

question:
For the following regular expression, select ALL inputs that match:

/^([a-z0-9\.\-]+)@([a-z]+)\.([a-z]{3})$/i
explanation: The domain part [a-z]+ allows letters only — no dots. bobjones@aut.ac.nz fails because 'aut.ac' contains a dot and 'nz' is only 2 chars (TLD needs exactly 3). The /i flag makes BOBJONES valid.
simple_explanation: The domain [a-z]+ means NO dots. aut.ac.nz has two dots — fails. Also the TLD must be exactly 3 letters so .nz (2 chars) fails too. /i makes uppercase work.

explanation: Step by step: start=1, +=5 → 6, *=3 → 18, /=2 → 9. Each compound operator applies to the current value.
simple_explanation: 1 + 5 = 6, then 6 × 3 = 18, then 18 ÷ 2 = 9. Work through each step in order.

question:
Which statements are TRUE about HTTP GET and POST methods?

Select ALL that apply.
options: GET requests are more secure than POST requests
POST requests only allow text data
GET requests are limited in their content length
GET requests transmit data as key-value pairs in the URL
POST requests are not cached by the browser
explanation: GET appends data to the URL (visible, limited to ~2048 chars). POST sends data in the request body (hidden, unlimited, supports binary). GET is LESS secure not more. POST can carry binary data, not just text.
simple_explanation: GET data goes in the URL — you can see it in the address bar and it has a length limit. POST hides data in the request body, has no length limit, and isn't cached.

question:
Which statements are TRUE about PHP variable names?

Select ALL that apply.
options: PHP variable names can include any symbol (@, #, !, etc.)
PHP variable names cannot start with a number
PHP variable names must begin with a dollar sign ($)
PHP variable names are not case sensitive
PHP variable names can include spaces
explanation: Variables must start with $ and the character after $ must be a letter or underscore (not a digit). They ARE case-sensitive ($Name ≠ $name). Spaces and special symbols are not allowed.
simple_explanation: Two rules: must start with $, and the first character after $ can't be a number. $name and $Name are completely different variables — PHP cares about case.

question:
How many of the following are syntactically correct HTML? (1)

Hello World!

(2)

Hello World!

(3) (4)

Hello World!
explanation:
(1) ✓ correct nesting. (2) ✗ attribute value must be quoted: class="text". (3) ✓ valid self-closing void element. (4) ✗ wrong closing order —

must come before .
simple_explanation: Attributes need quoted values (class=text should be class="text"). Tags must close in reverse order — open div then p, so close p then div. (1) and (3) are the only correct ones.

question: What is the default port number for TCP/IP HTTP servers?
explanation: Port 80 is HTTP. Port 443 is HTTPS. Port 8080 is an alternative HTTP port. Port 22 is SSH. When you visit http://example.com your browser connects on port 80 automatically.
simple_explanation: 80 = HTTP, 443 = HTTPS. You never type the port because browsers assume it. http:// → port 80, https:// → port 443.

question:
Given this HTML form:

<form action="login.php" method="post">
  <input type="text" name="username" />
</form>

In login.php, complete the code:

$username = ___['username'];
explanation: $_POST holds all values submitted via the POST method, keyed by the input's name attribute. Since method="post" and name="username", you access it as $_POST['username'].
simple_explanation: The form uses method=post so PHP puts the data in $_POST. The key is the input's name attribute. name="username" → $_POST['username'].

explanation: explode(";", $str) splits by semicolons: [0]=Nathan, [1]=Muhammad, [2]=Jane, [3]=Joshua, [4]=Jishen. Array indexes start at 0, so $names[2] = Jane.
simple_explanation: explode splits a string into an array. Arrays start at index 0, so index 2 is the third item = Jane.

question:
A student can enrol in many courses, and a course can have many students.

What is the correct database relationship type?
explanation: Many-to-Many: both sides can relate to multiple records on the other side. Implemented with a junction table (e.g. enrolments) containing student_id and course_id foreign keys.
simple_explanation: Many students can take many courses = Many-to-Many. In SQL you'd need a third linking table (like enrolments) to connect them.

question: Which correctly ranks CSS priority from HIGHEST to LOWEST?
options: Browser defaults → External stylesheet → Inline styles
Inline styles → External/Internal stylesheet → Browser defaults
External stylesheet → Inline styles → Browser defaults
Browser defaults → Inline styles → External stylesheet
explanation: Priority (high to low): Inline styles (on the element) → External/Internal stylesheets → Browser defaults. Inline wins because it is the most specific. !important can override this order.
simple_explanation: Inline styles always win because they're the most specific — right there on the element. Browser defaults always lose. External CSS files are in the middle.

question:
Given this PHP array, what is the value of $arr[2][1]?

$arr = array(
  array(1, 5, 9),
  array(2, 6, 10),
  array(3, 7, 11),
  array(4, 8, 12)
);
explanation: $arr[2] = third row (0-indexed) = array(3, 7, 11). [1] = second element = 7. Arrays always start at index 0.
simple_explanation: $arr[2] = third row = (3, 7, 11). Then [1] = second item = 7. Remember: counting starts at 0.

question:
Which CSS selector correctly targets all

elements inside

elements?
explanation: div.content means 'a div element that has class content'. Adding p selects all p descendants inside it. .content div p would look for p inside div inside any .content element. div p.content selects p elements with class=content inside a div.
simple_explanation: div.content = a div with class=content. Then p = all p tags inside that div. It reads like a sentence: find div with class content, then find p inside it.
